food_name: Pig Stomach Soup (Bak Kut Teh Style)
food_subgroup: Pork offal soup; braised or simmered pig stomach in herbal broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for pig stomach soup in Bak Kut Teh style; closest parent 'pork stomach food product' used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers pig stomach as an ingredient; no specific soup preparation code exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for pig stomach soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pig stomach soup as served in a Malaysian or Singaporean Bak Kut Teh restaurant, approximately 350 g including broth and solid pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Pork stomach, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10193); supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for soup preparation adjustment
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork stomach (NDB 10193). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce and herbal broth contribution typical of Bak Kut Teh preparation, consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition guidance for pork offal soups. Energy and macros reflect the mixed dish including broth dilution at approximately 40% solid, 60% broth by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on pork-based meat soup with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pig stomach soup. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous pork-based meat soups with low carbohydrate content from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (30 × 1.8 × 3.5) / 100 = 1.89, rounded to 1.9. The low GI reflects the negligible starch content of this predominantly protein-and-fat dish.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and herbal broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl and request reduced soy sauce, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig stomach soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its low carbohydrate content (1.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated 30) produce a minimal glycemic load of approximately 1.9 per serving; however, sodium intake from the broth should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol and sodium; moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume pig stomach soup in moderation due to its cholesterol content (approximately 95 mg per 100 g) and high sodium load from the broth; choosing a lighter broth and smaller portions can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig Stomach Soup (Bak Kut Teh Style), known in Chinese as 猪肚汤 (Zhu Du Tang), is a traditional Malaysian and Singaporean pork offal soup dish in which cleaned and tenderised pig stomach is slow-simmered in a deeply aromatic herbal broth. The broth is typically prepared with white pepper, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and soy sauce, producing a rich, peppery, and slightly savoury flavour profile. The pig stomach itself has a firm yet yielding texture after prolonged braising, absorbing the herbal and peppery notes of the broth. The dish is commonly served with tofu puffs, dried mushrooms, and sometimes pork ribs, accompanied by steamed white rice or you tiao (fried dough fritters). Nutritionally, pig stomach soup is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with moderate fat content. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 72 kcal, 9.2 g protein, 3.1 g fat, and only 1.8 g carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Its glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), resulting in a negligible glycemic load per serving. The dish is high in sodium due to soy sauce and broth, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is a good source of B vitamins, particularly B12, riboflavin, and niacin, as well as zinc and iron from the pork offal. Pig stomach is not halal and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. In Singapore and Malaysia, this dish is a staple comfort food often consumed at breakfast or dinner in Bak Kut Teh restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pig stomach soup?
Is Bak Kut Teh pig stomach suitable for diabetics?
Is pig stomach soup high in sodium?
Can I eat pig stomach soup if I have high blood pressure?
What does pig stomach taste like?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork stomach data with broth dilution adjustment. No exact match for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at the time of processing. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable pork-based meat soups. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy pork stomach entry and is not reliably estimated for this soup preparation. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for cooked pork stomach. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for pork stomach. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible for this preparation based on USDA data. GI estimated at 30 (low) using analogous pork-based meat soup data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for pig stomach soup exists in the approved literature. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects broth contribution from soy sauce typical of Bak Kut Teh preparation and represents a moderate-to-high sodium dish. The food_name has been standardised to 'Pig Stomach Soup (Bak Kut Teh Style)' as the canonical English name; the input label 'M4 Pig Stomach 猪肚' is a menu code retained in aliases. This dish is not halal as it contains pork.
